Indicates whether this cost center is subject to SOX financial controls and audit requirements (True for publicly traded entities) or exempt (False).
The total square footage of physical space occupied by this cost center, used for space-based cost allocation and facility management analysis.

business_glossary_term: Uniform System of Accounts for the Lodging Industry (USALI) Department Code
description: The standard USALI departmental code aligning this cost center to the industry-standard chart of accounts for lodging P&L reporting (e.g., 10=Rooms, 20=Food, 30=Beverage, 40=Other Operated Departments, 50=Administrative & General).
Primary key for fiscal_period
Reference to the corresponding fiscal period in the prior year. Used for year-over-year variance analysis and comparative financial reporting.
Self-referencing FK on fiscal_period (prior_fiscal_period_id)
The budget version or scenario associated with this fiscal period (e.g., Original Budget, Revised Budget, Forecast). Used for budget vs actual variance analysis.
Total number of business days (excluding weekends and holidays) in this fiscal period. Used for operational metrics and productivity calculations.
The calendar month number (1-12) in which the period starts. Used for calendar-based reporting and seasonality analysis.
The calendar quarter number (1-4) in which the period starts. Used for calendar-based comparisons and external benchmarking.
The calendar year in which the period starts (e.g., 2024). May differ from fiscal_year for companies with non-calendar fiscal years.
The actual date when the fiscal period was closed for transaction posting. Null if period is still open. Used for financial close tracking and SOX compliance.
The timestamp when this fiscal period record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Total number of calendar days in this fiscal period. Used for daily average calculations (e.g., Average Daily Rate, Revenue Per Available Room).
The last calendar date included in this fiscal period. Defines the end boundary for transaction posting and financial reporting.
The fiscal month number within the fiscal year (1-12). Null for non-monthly periods. Used for monthly financial close and management reporting.
Additional descriptive text or notes about this fiscal period, including special circumstances, adjustments, or reporting considerations.

Current lifecycle status of the fiscal period. Open: period is active and transactions can be posted. Closed: period is closed for regular posting but can be reopened. Locked: period is finalized and cannot accept new transactions without special authorization. Archived: period is archived for historical reference only.
The fiscal quarter number within the fiscal year (1-4). Null for non-quarterly periods. Used for quarterly financial reporting and SEC filings.
The fiscal week number within the fiscal year (1-52 or 1-53). Null for non-weekly periods. Used for weekly operational and revenue reporting in hospitality operations.
The fiscal year to which this period belongs (e.g., 2024). Used for year-over-year comparisons and annual financial statement consolidation.
Indicates whether this is a special adjustment period (e.g., Period 13) used for year-end adjustments and accruals. True if adjustment period, False if regular operating period.
Indicates whether this fiscal period falls within a leap year. True if leap year, False otherwise. Used for accurate daily average calculations.
The date when the fiscal period was locked and finalized. Null if period is not yet locked. Used for audit trail and financial statement finalization.
The timestamp when this fiscal period record was last modified. Used for audit trail and change tracking.
Business identifier code for the fiscal period, typically formatted as YYYY-MM or YYYY-Pnn (e.g., 2024-01, 2024-P01). Used in financial reports and external communications.
Human-readable name of the fiscal period (e.g., January 2024, Q1 2024, FY2024-P01). Used for display in reports and dashboards.
Sequential period number within the fiscal year (e.g., 1-12 for monthly periods, 1-4 for quarterly periods). Used for period-to-period comparisons and trend analysis.

Classification of the fiscal period granularity: month (standard monthly period), quarter (quarterly period), year (annual fiscal year), week (weekly period for operational reporting), or custom (non-standard period for special reporting needs).
The date when a previously closed period was reopened for adjustments. Null if period has never been reopened. Used for audit trail and SOX compliance monitoring.
The three-letter ISO 4217 currency code used for financial reporting in this period (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP). Used for multi-currency consolidation.
The first calendar date included in this fiscal period. Defines the beginning of the period for transaction posting and financial reporting.
Primary key for gl_batch

Reference to the original GL batch that this batch reverses, if this is a reversal batch. Used to track the relationship between reversal entries and their original accruals. Null if this is not a reversal batch.
Reference to the GL batch that reverses this batch, if applicable. Used to track the relationship between original accrual entries and their subsequent reversals. Null if batch has not been reversed.
Self-referencing FK on gl_batch (reversal_gl_batch_id)
The fiscal period to which this GL batch applies, typically in format YYYY-MM or period number (e.g., '2024-01', 'Period 3 FY2024'). Used for period-end closing and financial reporting alignment.
Indicates whether this GL batch requires formal approval before it can be posted to the general ledger. True if approval workflow is required, False if batch can be posted without approval.
User ID or name of the person who approved the GL batch for posting. Required for SOX compliance and audit trail. Null if batch has not been approved or does not require approval.
Date and time when the GL batch was approved for posting. Part of the audit trail for SOX compliance. Null if batch has not been approved.
Descriptive name or title assigned to the GL batch to identify its purpose or content (e.g., 'Monthly Accruals - January 2024', 'Payroll Posting - Week 12').
Human-readable business identifier for the GL batch, typically assigned by the accounting system or finance team for tracking and reference purposes.

Current lifecycle status of the GL batch: draft (being prepared), pending_approval (submitted for review), approved (authorized for posting), posted (committed to the general ledger), rejected (not approved), or cancelled (voided before posting).

Classification of the GL batch based on its accounting purpose: standard (regular transactions), recurring (automated periodic entries), adjustment (correcting entries), accrual (period-end accruals), reversal (reversing prior entries), or closing (period/year-end closing entries).
Optional control total amount provided by the batch creator for validation purposes. Used to verify that the sum of batch entries matches the expected total before posting.
Date and time when the GL batch record was first created in the system. Part of the audit trail for tracking batch lifecycle.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code representing the currency in which the GL batch amounts are denominated (e.g., 'USD', 'EUR', 'GBP').
Code identifying the department or cost center associated with this GL batch (e.g., 'ROOMS', 'F&B', 'ADMIN'). Used for departmental accounting and GOP calculation per USALI.
The business date or transaction date that the GL batch entries represent. May differ from posting_date when backdating or forward-dating entries for accruals or adjustments.
Total number of individual journal entries or line items contained within this GL batch. Used for validation and reconciliation purposes.

External reference number or identifier from the source system or business process that originated this GL batch (e.g., invoice number, payroll run ID, bank statement reference). Used for cross-system reconciliation.
The fiscal year to which this GL batch belongs (e.g., 2024). Used for annual financial reporting and year-end closing processes.
Detailed textual description of the GL batch purpose, content, or context. Provides additional information beyond the batch name for audit trail and user reference.
Indicates whether the GL batch is in balance (total debits equal total credits). True if balanced, False if out of balance. Out-of-balance batches cannot be posted.
Indicates whether this GL batch is part of a recurring batch template that is automatically generated on a scheduled basis (e.g., monthly rent accruals, depreciation entries).
User ID or name of the person who last modified the GL batch. Part of the audit trail for SOX compliance. Null if batch has never been modified after creation.
Date and time when the GL batch was last modified. Part of the audit trail for tracking changes to the batch. Null if batch has never been modified after creation.
Free-form notes or comments added by users regarding the GL batch. Used for additional context, special instructions, or documentation of unusual circumstances.
User ID or name of the person who posted the GL batch to the general ledger. Required for audit trail and SOX compliance. Null if batch has not been posted.
Date and time when the GL batch was posted to the general ledger. Represents the moment the batch entries were committed to the GL. Null if batch has not been posted.
The date on which the GL batch was or will be posted to the general ledger. This is the effective date for financial reporting and impacts the accounting period in which transactions are recognized.
For recurring batches, specifies the frequency at which the batch is automatically generated: daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or annually. Null for non-recurring batches.
Textual explanation provided by the approver when rejecting a GL batch. Documents why the batch was not approved for posting. Null if batch has not been rejected.
The date on which this GL batch should be automatically reversed, if applicable. Commonly used for accrual entries that need to be reversed in the subsequent period.
Specific module or sub-system within the source system that generated the batch (e.g., 'Accounts Payable', 'Accounts Receivable', 'Fixed Assets', 'Payroll').
Sum of all credit entries in the GL batch. Must equal total_debit_amount for the batch to be in balance per double-entry accounting principles.
Sum of all debit entries in the GL batch. Must equal total_credit_amount for the batch to be in balance per double-entry accounting principles.
User ID or name of the person who created or initiated the GL batch. Part of the audit trail for SOX compliance and internal controls.
Unique identifier for the general ledger account. Primary key.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_center. Business justification: Some GL accounts have default cost centers for automatic allocation when no specific cost center is provided on transactions. The cost_center_code STRING should be replaced with a proper FK to cost_ce

Foreign key linking to finance.profit_center. Business justification: Some GL accounts have default profit centers for automatic allocation when no specific profit center is provided on transactions. The profit_center_code STRING should be replaced with a proper FK to p
The unique identifier of the property to which this ledger account belongs. Supports property-level chart of accounts and multi-property financial consolidation. Links to the property master entity.

The USALI-defined category grouping for the account (e.g., Rooms, Food and Beverage, Other Operated Departments, Administrative and General, Sales and Marketing, Property Operations and Maintenance, Utilities, Fixed Charges). Used for departmental P&L reporting and GOP calculation.
The descriptive name of the general ledger account (e.g., Room Revenue, Food and Beverage Cost of Sales, Payroll and Related Expenses, Property Operations and Maintenance).
The unique account number assigned to this general ledger account per USALI chart of accounts structure. Typically 4-10 digit numeric code used for financial reporting and transaction posting.

The detailed subcategory within the USALI account category (e.g., within Food and Beverage: Restaurant Revenue, Banquet Revenue, Room Service Revenue; within Rooms: Payroll and Related, Guest Supplies, Laundry and Linen). Enables granular financial analysis.
The fundamental accounting classification of the ledger account: asset, liability, equity, revenue, or expense. Determines placement in financial statements and normal balance behavior.
Indicates whether detailed audit trail documentation is required for all transactions posted to this account. Used for high-risk accounts, regulatory compliance, and forensic accounting.

The section of the balance sheet where this account appears (current assets, non-current assets, current liabilities, non-current liabilities, equity). Determines liquidity classification and working capital calculation.
The cash flow statement category for this account: operating activities, investing activities, or financing activities. Used for automated cash flow statement preparation per GAAP/IFRS.
The account number used for consolidation at the corporate or regional level. Maps property-level accounts to standardized corporate chart of accounts for multi-property financial consolidation.
The timestamp when this ledger account record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
The three-letter ISO 4217 currency code in which this ledger account is denominated (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP). Supports multi-currency accounting for global property portfolios.
The date on which this ledger account becomes inactive and no longer accepts new transactions. Null for accounts with no planned end date. Used for account retirement and chart of accounts cleanup.
The date from which this ledger account becomes active and available for transaction posting. Supports phased chart of accounts implementation and account structure changes.

The functional area classification for financial statement presentation (e.g., Operating Revenue, Cost of Sales, Operating Expenses, Fixed Charges). Used for USALI-compliant income statement formatting and GOP calculation.

The section of the income statement where this account appears per USALI format (e.g., Operated Departments Revenue, Operated Departments Expenses, Undistributed Operating Expenses, Fixed Charges). Supports GOP, EBITDA, and NOI calculation.
Indicates whether the ledger account is currently active and available for transaction posting. Inactive accounts are retained for historical reporting but cannot accept new transactions.

Indicates whether this is a control account (summary account) that aggregates balances from subsidiary ledgers (e.g., Accounts Receivable control account summarizing individual guest folios). Control accounts do not accept direct postings.

Indicates whether this account is used for intercompany transactions between properties or legal entities within the hospitality group. Intercompany accounts are eliminated during consolidated financial statement preparation.
Indicates whether this account requires periodic reconciliation (e.g., bank accounts, accounts receivable, inventory accounts). Used for internal control and SOX compliance.

Indicates whether this is a statistical account used for non-monetary metrics (e.g., room nights sold, covers served, occupied room count). Statistical accounts support KPI calculation (ADR, RevPAR, GOPPAR) but do not carry monetary balances.
The date of the most recent transaction posted to this account. Used for dormant account identification and account usage analysis.
Extended description of the ledger account purpose, usage guidelines, and posting rules. Provides additional context beyond the account name for accounting staff and auditors.
The timestamp when this ledger account record was last modified. Used for change tracking and audit trail.

Indicates whether the account normally carries a debit or credit balance. Assets and expenses have debit normal balances; liabilities, equity, and revenue have credit normal balances. Used for transaction validation and financial statement preparation.
Free-form notes field for special instructions, reconciliation notes, or account-specific documentation. Used by accounting staff for operational guidance and audit documentation.

Indicates whether direct posting to this account is blocked. Posting blocks are used for control accounts, summary accounts, or accounts under investigation. Transactions must be posted to subsidiary accounts instead.

Indicates whether this account is subject to SOX internal control testing and documentation requirements. Typically true for material accounts affecting financial statement accuracy for publicly traded hospitality companies.

The tax category or tax line item code associated with this account for tax reporting purposes (e.g., taxable revenue, non-taxable revenue, deductible expense, non-deductible expense). Supports automated tax return preparation.
Unique identifier for the profit center. Primary key for this entity.
Foreign key reference to the primary cost center associated with this profit center for expense allocation and departmental accounting per USALI standards.
Foreign key reference to the employee responsible for managing this profit center. Used for accountability reporting and performance evaluation.
Foreign key reference to the legal ownership entity responsible for this profit center. Supports consolidated financial statements across the portfolio and inter-company eliminations.

Foreign key reference to the parent profit center in a hierarchical profit center structure. Enables roll-up reporting for complex properties with nested profit centers (e.g., a resort with multiple F&B (Food and Beverage) outlets).

Foreign key reference to the parent property or resort to which this profit center belongs. Enables property-level P&L (Profit and Loss) consolidation and RevPAR (Revenue Per Available Room) contribution analysis.
Foreign key reference to the primary revenue center for this profit center. Used for revenue recognition and departmental revenue reporting per USALI.
Primary street address line for the profit center's physical location. Required for property-level profit centers and standalone F&B (Food and Beverage) or event venues.
Secondary address line for suite, floor, or building information.
Brand or franchise affiliation code for the profit center (e.g., Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt, independent). Used for brand-level performance reporting and franchise fee calculations.

SAP Business Area code for cross-company-code segment reporting. Enables consolidated reporting across multiple legal entities within the same business segment (e.g., luxury hotels, select-service properties).
City or municipality where the profit center is located.
The date the profit center was permanently closed. Used for historical analysis and asset disposition reporting.
SAP Company Code representing the legal entity for external financial reporting. Links profit center results to the general ledger and statutory financial statements.
SAP Controlling Area code to which this profit center is assigned. Defines the organizational boundary for internal cost and revenue accounting.
ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code for the profit center's location. Used for multi-country consolidation and compliance with local regulatory reporting requirements.
Timestamp when this profit center record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking per SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act) compliance requirements.
ISO 4217 three-letter currency code for the profit center's local currency. Used for multi-currency consolidation and foreign exchange translation per IFRS/GAAP.
The date on which this profit center ceased operations or was closed. Null for currently active profit centers. Used for historical reporting and closed property analysis.
The date from which this profit center became active and began contributing to financial statements. Used for time-based reporting and historical analysis.
Primary email address for business correspondence related to this profit center. Organizational contact data classified as confidential.
ISO 4217 three-letter functional currency code for financial consolidation. May differ from local currency for properties operating in high-inflation or multi-currency environments.
The level of this profit center within the profit center hierarchy (1 = top-level property, 2 = department, 3 = sub-department, etc.). Used for recursive roll-up calculations.
Boolean flag indicating whether this profit center's financial results are included in consolidated financial statements. Used for inter-company elimination and segment reporting per IFRS/GAAP.

Boolean flag indicating whether this profit center directly generates guest-facing revenue (true) or is a support/overhead center (false). Used for GOP (Gross Operating Profit) and departmental profit analysis per USALI.
Timestamp of the most recent modification to this profit center record. Used for change tracking and audit compliance per SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act).
The original opening or launch date of the profit center. Used for property age analysis and lifecycle reporting.
Primary contact phone number for the profit center. Organizational contact data classified as confidential.
Postal or ZIP code for the profit center's address.

The externally-known unique business identifier for the profit center used in financial reporting and consolidation. Typically alphanumeric code assigned per SAP S/4HANA Profit Center Accounting standards.
Detailed business description of the profit center, including its purpose, services offered, and unique characteristics. Used for internal documentation and reporting context.
The full business name of the profit center (e.g., 'Grand Plaza Hotel Downtown', 'Rooftop Bar & Lounge', 'Convention Center Ballroom A').
Current lifecycle status of the profit center. Active centers contribute to consolidated financial statements; inactive or closed centers are excluded from operational reporting.

Classification of the profit center by business unit type. Determines the applicable revenue and cost accounting rules per USALI departmental structure. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: property|fnb_outlet|event_venue|spa|retail|parking|other — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

Total number of guest rooms or keys available at this profit center (applicable only to property-level profit centers). Used for RevPAR (Revenue Per Available Room), GOPPAR (Gross Operating Profit Per Available Room), and occupancy calculations.
Maximum seating capacity for F&B (Food and Beverage) outlets or event venues. Used for revenue per seat analysis and capacity utilization reporting.

Business segment classification for the profit center (e.g., luxury, premium, select-service). Aligns with IFRS 8 segment reporting requirements and STR (Smith Travel Research) competitive set definitions.
Abbreviated or short name for the profit center used in reports and dashboards where space is limited.
Total square footage of the profit center's physical space. Used for revenue per square foot analysis and space utilization metrics.

Official star rating or quality classification for the profit center (e.g., 3.5, 4.0, 5.0). Used for competitive set analysis and ADR (Average Daily Rate) benchmarking per STR (Smith Travel Research) standards.
ISO 3166-2 state or province code for the profit center's location. Used for tax jurisdiction determination and regulatory reporting.
Tax jurisdiction code for sales tax, VAT (Value-Added Tax), or GST (Goods and Services Tax) reporting. Used for compliance with local tax regulations and automated tax calculation.
Unique identifier for the budget line item. Primary key.

Reference to the parent budget header to which this line item belongs.

Reference to the property for which this budget line is planned. Enables property-level budget tracking and consolidation.
The driver or basis used for allocation if allocation_method is driver-based. Examples include room nights, square footage, headcount, or revenue percentage.
The method used to allocate this budget line to the cost center or profit center. Supports transparency in budget allocation logic.
The current approval status of this budget line. Supports budget workflow and SOX financial controls.
The name or identifier of the individual who approved this budget line. Supports audit trail and SOX compliance.
The date and time when this budget line was approved. Supports audit trail and SOX compliance.
High-level classification of the budget line. Distinguishes between revenue, operating expenses, fixed expenses, and capital expenditures per USALI standards.
The name or identifier of the individual or role responsible for this budget line. Supports accountability and budget governance.
The type of budget this line belongs to. Supports multiple budget scenarios for planning and variance analysis.
The version identifier for this budget line. Supports multiple budget scenarios such as original budget, revised budget, forecast, or rolling forecast.
The date and time when this budget line record was first created in the system. Supports audit trail and data lineage.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the planned amount. Supports multi-currency budget planning and consolidation.
The department code to which this budget line is allocated. Aligns with USALI departmental structure such as Rooms, Food and Beverage, Sales and Marketing, Property Operations and Maintenance.
The date until which this budget line remains effective. Null indicates the budget line is currently active.
The date from which this budget line becomes effective. Supports temporal budget management and mid-year budget changes.
The fiscal period (month) within the fiscal year for which this budget line is planned. Typically 1-12 for monthly periods, or 1-13 for 13-period calendars.
The fiscal year for which this budget line is planned. Four-digit year representation.
The date and time when this budget line record was last modified. Supports audit trail and change tracking.
Sequential line item number within the parent budget. Used for ordering and referencing individual budget lines.
Indicates whether this budget line is locked from further edits. True if locked, False if editable. Supports budget freeze and SOX controls.
Free-text notes or comments providing additional context or justification for this budget line. Used for management review and audit trail.
The budgeted monetary amount for this GL account, cost center, profit center, and fiscal period combination. Represents the planned expense or revenue target.
The actual amount from the prior fiscal year for the same GL account, cost center, and fiscal period. Used for year-over-year budget comparison and trend analysis.
The planned quantity associated with this budget line, if applicable. Used for volume-based budgeting such as room nights, covers, or headcount.
The unique identifier of this budget line in the source system. Supports data lineage and reconciliation.
The unit of measure for the budget quantity field. Examples include ROOM_NIGHTS, COVERS, FTE (Full-Time Equivalent), UNITS.
The planned unit price or rate used to calculate the planned amount. Derived from planned_amount divided by quantity.
The acceptable variance threshold percentage for this budget line. Used to flag significant budget-vs-actual variances for management review.

Identifier of the employee who approved the capital expenditure request. Null if not yet approved.
Identifier of the employee who initiated the capital expenditure request.
Identifier of the property or hotel location for which the capital expenditure is requested.
Self-referencing FK on capex_request (revised_capex_request_id)
Identifier of the primary vendor or contractor selected to execute the capital expenditure project. Null if not yet selected.
Actual date when the capital expenditure project was completed and the asset placed in service. Null if not yet completed.
Actual date when the capital expenditure project commenced. Null if not yet started.
Level of management authority required to approve this capital expenditure request based on amount thresholds and policy.
Date when the capital expenditure request was approved by authorized management. Null if not yet approved.
Total capital expenditure amount approved by management, which may differ from the requested amount. Null if not yet approved.
Full name of the employee who approved the capital expenditure request. Null if not yet approved.
Classification of the asset type being acquired or improved through this capital expenditure.
Fiscal year for which the capital expenditure is budgeted and planned.
Rationale and business case for the capital expenditure, including strategic alignment and expected benefits.
Timestamp when the capital expenditure request record was first created in the system.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the requested and approved amounts.
Code identifying the department or cost center requesting the capital expenditure, aligned with USALI departmental structure.
Accounting method to be used for depreciating the capital asset over its useful life.
Assessment level of environmental impact associated with the capital expenditure project, supporting sustainability reporting.
Estimated useful life of the asset in years for depreciation calculation purposes.
General ledger account code to which the capital expenditure will be capitalized, following the chart of accounts structure.
Indicates whether the capital expenditure project spans multiple fiscal years requiring phased budgeting and approval.
Timestamp when the capital expenditure request record was last modified or updated.
Additional comments, notes, or supplementary information related to the capital expenditure request.
Estimated number of months required to recover the capital expenditure investment through generated cash flows or savings.
Planned date for the capital expenditure project to be completed and the asset placed in service.
Planned date for the capital expenditure project to commence.
Business priority assigned to the capital expenditure request based on urgency and strategic importance.
Detailed narrative description of the capital expenditure project, including scope, objectives, and expected outcomes.
Descriptive name of the capital expenditure project or initiative.
Purchase order number issued for the capital expenditure procurement. Null if not yet issued.
Explanation provided when a capital expenditure request is rejected by approving authority. Null if not rejected.
Date when the capital expenditure request was formally submitted for review.
Business-facing unique identifier for the capital expenditure request, formatted as CAPEX-YYYY-NNNNNN.
Current lifecycle status of the capital expenditure request in the approval workflow.
Classification of the capital expenditure request: CapEx (general capital expenditure), FF&E (furniture, fixtures, and equipment), renovation, expansion, maintenance, technology, or equipment.
Total capital expenditure amount requested for the project in the property's functional currency.
Full name of the employee who initiated the capital expenditure request.
Estimated return on investment percentage for the capital expenditure project, used in financial justification.
Indicates whether this capital expenditure request is subject to SOX financial controls and audit requirements for publicly traded entities.
Unique identifier for the finance budget record. Primary key.

Reference to the property for which this budget applies. Links to property master data.
The planned or forecasted monetary amount for this budget line item. Represents the target financial value for the specified GL account, cost center, and fiscal period combination.
Name or identifier of the executive or committee who approved this budget. Supports SOX financial controls and audit trail.
The date on which this budget was formally approved. Critical for audit trail and SOX compliance.

High-level classification of the budget line: revenue (rooms, F&B, events), operating expense (utilities, supplies), labor expense (wages, benefits), capital expenditure (CapEx), or furniture, fixtures, and equipment (FF&E) reserve.
Descriptive name of the budget (e.g., 'FY2024 Annual Operating Budget', 'Q2 2024 Reforecast').
Externally-known unique identifier for this budget document. Used for reference in management reports and approvals.

Current lifecycle status of the budget: draft (in preparation), submitted (awaiting approval), under review (being evaluated), approved (finalized), active (in use), closed (period ended), or superseded (replaced by newer version). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: draft|submitted|under_review|approved|active|closed|superseded — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Classification of the budget by planning cycle: annual operating budget, quarterly forecast, monthly budget, rolling forecast, reforecast, or capital expenditure budget.
Forecasted Average Daily Rate for the budget period. Calculated as rooms revenue divided by rooms sold. Key rooms division KPI.
Forecasted total number of room nights available for sale during the budget period. Accounts for planned renovations and out-of-order rooms.
Forecasted total number of F&B covers (guests served) for the budget period. Key volume metric for food and beverage operations.
Forecasted Cost Per Occupied Room for the budget period. Calculated as total departmental expenses divided by rooms sold. Measures cost efficiency.

business_glossary_term: Budgeted Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EBITDA)
description: Forecasted EBITDA for the budget period. Represents operating profitability before financing and non-cash charges.
Forecasted total events and meetings revenue for the budget period. Includes MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, Exhibitions) revenue.
Forecasted total food and beverage revenue for the budget period. Includes restaurant, bar, banquet, and room service revenue.
Forecasted Gross Operating Profit for the budget period. Calculated as total revenue minus departmental expenses and undistributed operating expenses. Key profitability metric per USALI.

business_glossary_term: Budgeted Gross Operating Profit Per Available Room (GOPPAR)
description: Forecasted Gross Operating Profit Per Available Room for the budget period. Calculated as GOP divided by available rooms. Measures profitability efficiency.
Forecasted total labor costs including wages, salaries, benefits, and payroll taxes for the budget period.
Forecasted Net Operating Income for the budget period. Represents property-level profitability after all operating expenses but before debt service and taxes.
Forecasted occupancy rate as a percentage for the budget period. Calculated as rooms sold divided by rooms available. Key demand metric.
Forecasted total operating expenses excluding labor for the budget period. Includes utilities, supplies, maintenance, and other departmental expenses.
Forecasted revenue from other sources (spa, parking, telecommunications, etc.) for the budget period.
Forecasted Revenue Per Available Room for the budget period. Calculated as rooms revenue divided by available rooms, or ADR multiplied by occupancy rate. Primary revenue performance metric.
Forecasted total number of room nights sold for the budget period. Key volume metric for rooms division.
Forecasted total rooms revenue for the budget period. Key metric for rooms division performance planning.

The credit or debit card brand used for the payment. Applicable only for card-based payment methods. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: visa|mastercard|amex|discover|jcb|diners|unionpay — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
The name of the cardholder as it appears on the payment card. Used for verification and reconciliation purposes.
The check number for check-based payments. Used for tracking and reconciliation with bank deposits.
The timestamp when this payment record was first created in the system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for audit trail and SOX compliance.
The date on which the payment was deposited to the property bank account. Used for cash flow tracking and bank reconciliation.
The foreign exchange rate applied to convert the payment currency to the property base currency. Used for multi-currency payment processing.
Boolean flag indicating whether this payment is an advance deposit received prior to guest arrival or service delivery. Used for revenue recognition timing.
Boolean flag indicating whether this payment represents a refund to the guest or customer. True if refund, False if collection.
The timestamp when this payment record was last modified. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for audit trail and change tracking per SOX requirements.
The net amount received after deducting transaction fees. Represents actual cash collected by the property.
The gross amount received in the payment transaction, in the payment currency. Represents the total collected before any adjustments or fees.
The interface or touchpoint through which the payment was received. Includes front desk, online portal, mobile app, call center, self-service kiosk, or mail.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the payment amount. Indicates the currency in which the payment was received.
The business date on which the payment was received or posted. Used for revenue recognition and cash flow reporting per USALI standards.

The instrument or mechanism used to make the payment. Includes credit card, debit card, cash, check, wire transfer, ACH, or direct bill settlement. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: credit_card|debit_card|cash|check|wire_transfer|ach|direct_bill — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Free-text notes or comments about the payment transaction. Used to capture special instructions, exceptions, or additional context.
Externally visible unique payment reference number used for tracking and reconciliation. Generated by PMS or financial system.

Current lifecycle status of the payment transaction. Tracks progression from authorization through settlement or failure. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: pending|authorized|captured|settled|failed|reversed|refunded — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
The precise date and time when the payment transaction was recorded in the system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
External reference number provided by the payer or payment processor. Used for cross-referencing with external systems and statements.
The reason for payment reversal if the payment status is reversed. Includes chargeback, error correction, guest dispute, etc.
The date on which the payment processor settled funds to the property account. Used for reconciliation with processor settlement reports.
Reference to the cashier shift during which the payment was processed. Used for shift reconciliation and cash drawer balancing.
Processing fee charged by payment processor or financial institution for the transaction. Includes credit card merchant fees, wire transfer fees, etc.
Unique transaction identifier assigned by the payment processor or gateway. Used for tracking and reconciliation with processor settlement reports.
The portion of the payment amount that has not yet been applied to specific AR invoices. Represents credit on account or overpayment.

Primary key for consent
Identifier of the hotel brand or sub-brand for which this consent applies. Allows brand-specific consent management in multi-brand hospitality groups.
Identifier of the marketing campaign that prompted this consent capture. Null if consent was not captured in the context of a specific campaign.
Identifier of the employee or system user who captured or recorded the consent on behalf of the guest (e.g., front desk agent, call center representative). Null if self-service.
Unique identifier of the guest to whom this consent record applies. Links to the guest master record in Salesforce CRM and OPERA PMS guest profiles.
Identifier of the loyalty program associated with this consent. Consent may be tied to loyalty membership enrollment or benefits.
Identifier of the specific property or hotel where the consent was captured. Null if consent applies across all properties or was captured centrally.
The guest preferred frequency for receiving marketing communications. Allows guests to control volume of messages even when opted in.
The date and time when the guest provided or updated their consent for this communication type. Critical for GDPR and CCPA compliance audit trails.

Current status of the guest consent for this communication channel. Opted-in indicates active consent, opted-out indicates explicit refusal, pending indicates consent request awaiting response, expired indicates consent that has lapsed per retention policy, and withdrawn indicates previously granted consent that has been revoked.

The type of marketing communication or data usage for which consent is being recorded. Includes email marketing, SMS marketing, push notifications, direct mail, third-party sharing, and phone call marketing.
The date and time when this consent record was first created in the system. Part of standard audit trail.
Indicates whether the guest has accepted the data processing agreement or privacy policy associated with this consent. Required for GDPR compliance.

Indicates whether the guest confirmed their consent through a double opt-in process (e.g., clicking a confirmation link in an email). True if confirmed, false if single opt-in only. Best practice for email marketing compliance.
The date and time when the guest confirmed their consent via double opt-in process. Null if double opt-in was not used or not yet confirmed.
The date on which this consent expires and must be re-confirmed. Some jurisdictions require periodic re-consent. Null if consent does not expire.
The IP address from which the consent was provided. Used for fraud detection and consent verification. May be considered PII under GDPR.
Indicates whether this consent record is currently active and should be honored for marketing operations. False if superseded by a newer consent record or if consent has been withdrawn.

The legal jurisdiction or country under which this consent record is governed. Determines applicable privacy regulations (GDPR for EU, CCPA for California, etc.). Uses ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country codes. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: USA|GBR|DEU|FRA|CAN|AUS|JPN|CHN|IND|BRA|MEX|ESP|ITA|NLD|SWE — 15 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

The language in which the consent was presented to and acknowledged by the guest. Uses ISO 639-3 language codes. Required for GDPR compliance to demonstrate informed consent. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: eng|spa|fra|deu|jpn|zho|ita|por|rus|ara|kor|nld|swe|dan|nor — 15 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
The date of the most recent marketing communication sent to the guest under this consent. Used to track engagement and enforce quiet periods.
The date and time when this consent record was last updated. Tracks all modifications to consent status, preferences, or metadata.
The legal basis under GDPR for processing this guest data for marketing purposes. Consent is most common for marketing; legitimate interest may apply for existing customer communications.
Indicates whether parental or guardian consent is required for this guest due to age restrictions under COPPA or GDPR. True if guest is a minor and special consent rules apply.
The date and time when the guest opted out or withdrew their consent for this communication type. Null if consent has never been withdrawn.
Free-text explanation or categorized reason provided by the guest for opting out of marketing communications. Used for service recovery and marketing strategy refinement.
Indicates whether parental or guardian consent has been verified for a minor guest. True if verified, false if not yet verified or not applicable.
The URL of the preference center or web form where the guest can update their consent preferences. Supports self-service consent management.
URL or storage path to the archived proof of consent (e.g., screenshot of form submission, signed document, recorded call). Required for regulatory audits.
The unique identifier of the corresponding consent record in Salesforce CRM. Enables bi-directional synchronization between lakehouse and operational CRM system.
Description of the scope of data usage covered by this consent. Specifies what data will be used and for what marketing purposes. Free-text field for transparency.

The channel or touchpoint through which the guest provided their consent. Examples include booking engine during reservation, loyalty program enrollment, front desk check-in, website form, mobile app, email preference link, call center interaction, or property self-service kiosk. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: booking_engine|loyalty_enrollment|front_desk|web_form|mobile_app|email_link|call_center|property_kiosk — 8 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Name of the third-party data processor or marketing platform that will process guest data under this consent (e.g., email service provider, SMS gateway). Required for GDPR transparency.
The browser or application user agent string captured at the time of consent. Provides technical context for consent capture and helps verify authenticity.
Version identifier of the consent policy or terms and conditions that the guest agreed to. Tracks changes to privacy policies and consent language over time.
Unique identifier for each guest communication record. Primary key for the guest communication entity.
Identifier of the marketing campaign under which this communication was sent. Links to the campaign master record.

Foreign key linking to reservation.cancellation. Business justification: Service recovery and win-back communications are triggered by cancellations. Marketing tracks cancellation-specific outreach for sentiment analysis, re-booking campaigns, and loyalty retention. Essent
Identifier of the communication template used for this message. Links to the template library for reusable content governance.

Foreign key linking to compliance.privacy_incident. Business justification: When guest communications are involved in data breaches (email list exposure, unauthorized sends to opted-out guests), linking communication records to privacy incidents enables impact assessment, sub
Identifier of the guest who received this communication. Links to the guest master record in the Guest domain.
Identifier of the property or brand associated with this communication. Used for property-specific or brand-specific messaging.
Identifier of the reservation associated with this communication, if applicable. Used for pre-arrival, in-stay, and post-stay communications.
The variant identifier if this communication was part of an A/B or multivariate test. Used for campaign optimization and performance analysis. Null if not part of a test.
The full text or HTML content of the communication message. Contains the complete message body including personalized merge fields and dynamic content.
The reason code or message explaining why the communication bounced. Examples include invalid email address, mailbox full, spam filter rejection, or server error. Null if no bounce occurred.
The date and time when the communication bounced or failed delivery. Null if successfully delivered.

The delivery channel through which the communication was sent. Email is the primary digital channel; SMS for text messages; push for mobile app notifications; direct mail for physical correspondence; in-app for in-application messages; WhatsApp for messaging app communications.
The date and time when the guest first clicked a link within the communication. Null if no clicks occurred or tracking not available.

Classification of the communication content type. Promotional includes offers and campaigns; transactional includes booking confirmations and receipts; loyalty includes program updates; survey includes NPS and GSS feedback requests; event includes meeting and conference invitations; service recovery includes apology and compensation messages.

Boolean indicator of whether the guest completed the desired action after receiving this communication (e.g., made a booking, redeemed an offer, completed a survey). True if conversion occurred; False otherwise.
The date and time when the guest completed the conversion action attributed to this communication. Null if no conversion occurred.

The monetary value of the conversion attributed to this communication, typically in the property's base currency. Used for calculating campaign ROI and guest acquisition cost. Null if no conversion or non-monetary conversion.
The date and time when this communication record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage.
The unique activity or task identifier in Salesforce CRM that corresponds to this communication. Used for cross-system reconciliation and guest interaction history.
The date and time when the communication was successfully delivered to the recipient's mail server or device. Null if delivery failed or is pending.

Current delivery status of the communication. Sent indicates the message left the system; delivered confirms receipt by the guest's mail server or device; bounced indicates delivery failure; failed indicates system error; unsubscribed indicates the guest opted out; blocked indicates spam filter rejection.
Two-letter ISO 639-1 language code indicating the language in which the communication was sent. Examples: en for English, es for Spanish, fr for French, de for German, zh for Chinese.
Locale code combining language and region for localization purposes. Format: language_REGION (e.g., en_US, en_GB, es_MX, fr_CA). Used for region-specific content and formatting.
The date and time when the guest first opened or viewed the communication. Tracked via pixel or read receipt. Null if not opened or tracking not available.

The guest's consent status for receiving this type of communication at the time of send. Opted_in indicates explicit consent; opted_out indicates withdrawal of consent; pending indicates consent requested but not confirmed; not_applicable for transactional communications that do not require opt-in.
JSON or delimited string containing the dynamic merge fields and their values used to personalize this communication. Examples: guest name, loyalty tier, reservation dates, property name.
The email address to which the communication was sent. Captured at send time to preserve historical accuracy even if the guest's current email changes.
The phone number to which the communication was sent, applicable for SMS and WhatsApp channels. Captured at send time to preserve historical accuracy.
The email address to which guest replies should be directed. May differ from sender email to route responses to appropriate teams.
The priority level assigned to this communication for send queue processing. High priority for time-sensitive transactional messages; normal for standard campaigns; low for bulk promotional sends.
The email address from which the communication was sent. Used for reply-to and sender identification. Must comply with CAN-SPAM sender requirements.
The display name of the sender as shown to the recipient. Examples: property name, brand name, or generic sender like 'Guest Services' or 'Reservations Team'.
The date and time when the communication was sent from the marketing system. This is the principal business event timestamp for the communication lifecycle.
The subject line or headline of the communication. For email, this is the email subject; for SMS, this may be the first line; for push notifications, this is the notification title.

The reason why this communication was suppressed or not sent, if applicable. Examples: guest on do-not-contact list, invalid contact information, frequency cap exceeded, unsubscribed from category. Null if communication was sent.

Boolean indicator of whether open and click tracking was enabled for this communication. True if tracking pixels and link tracking were included; False if tracking was disabled for privacy or technical reasons.
The date and time when the guest unsubscribed or opted out from this communication or communication type. Null if guest remains subscribed.
The date and time when this communication record was last modified. Updated when delivery status changes or engagement events occur.

The UTM medium parameter identifying the marketing medium (e.g., cpc, email, banner, social). Part of the standard UTM tracking taxonomy.
The UTM source parameter identifying the traffic source (e.g., google, facebook, newsletter). Part of the standard UTM tracking taxonomy.

Unique identifier for each offer redemption transaction. Primary key for the offer redemption record.

Reference to the guest loyalty program membership if the redemption was tied to a loyalty member benefit or points redemption.

Reference to the guest who redeemed the promotional offer.
Reference to the hotel property where the offer was redeemed.
Reference to the reservation associated with this offer redemption, if redeemed at point of booking. Null if redeemed at point of sale.
Reference to the travel agent or OTA partner if the offer was redeemed through an intermediary channel.

The date when the reservation was booked, if this redemption occurred at point of booking. Used for lead time and booking window analysis.
The date and time when this offer redemption record was first created in the system. Audit trail for data lineage.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the redemption transaction amounts.
The type of device used by the guest to redeem the offer. Enables device-specific campaign optimization.
The monetary value of the discount applied through the promotional offer redemption. Calculated as original rate minus final rate.
The percentage discount applied if the offer type is percentage-based. Null for non-percentage offers.
The type of discount or benefit provided by the promotional offer. Enables segmentation of offer performance by benefit type.

The IP address from which the offer redemption was initiated. Used for fraud detection and geographic analysis.
The number of nights for the reservation associated with this offer redemption. Used for LOS restriction compliance and performance analysis.
The market segment classification for this redemption transaction. Used for segmentation analysis and revenue management.
The promotional code entered or applied by the guest at redemption. Used for tracking campaign performance and fraud prevention.
The original rate or price before the promotional offer discount was applied. Used to calculate discount value and campaign ROI.
The number of loyalty points earned from this transaction after offer redemption, if the offer includes a points multiplier benefit.
The number of loyalty points redeemed as part of this offer, if applicable. Null for non-points-based offers.
Indicates whether the guest explicitly accepted the promotional offer terms and conditions at redemption. Required for compliance and dispute resolution.
The rate plan code applied to the reservation after offer redemption. Links to the promotional rate plan in the PMS.

The geographic latitude coordinate where the offer was redeemed, if captured. Used for location-based campaign analysis.
The geographic longitude coordinate where the offer was redeemed, if captured. Used for location-based campaign analysis.
The exact date and time when the offer was redeemed by the guest. Primary business event timestamp for this transaction.
Detailed explanation if the offer redemption was rejected. Includes reasons such as expired offer, invalid code, terms not met, or fraud detection.
The room type code for the reservation associated with this offer redemption. Used to track offer performance by room category.
The booking source code indicating where the guest originated. Used for attribution and channel performance analysis.
The check-in date for the reservation associated with this offer redemption. Null if redeemed at point of sale.
The check-out date for the reservation associated with this offer redemption. Null if redeemed at point of sale.
The date and time when the guest accepted the promotional offer terms and conditions.

The browser or application user agent string captured at redemption. Used for technical analytics and fraud detection.

Primary key for survey_program

Foreign key linking to marketing.brand. Business justification: survey_program currently has brand_code (STRING) which is a denormalized reference to brand. Adding brand_id FK to brand.marketing_brand_id normalizes this relationship and allows proper referential i
Foreign key reference to the parent marketing campaign if this survey is part of a broader campaign initiative. Null for standalone survey programs.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_center. Business justification: Guest satisfaction survey programs (Medallia, Qualtrics) have vendor subscription costs, incentive expenses, and operational costs that must be allocated to marketing cost centers for budget tracking
Foreign key reference to the marketing user or team responsible for managing this survey program. Links to workforce/user management system.
Foreign key reference to the specific property when property_scope is 'property'. Null for multi-property or enterprise-wide surveys.
Foreign key reference to the user who last modified the survey program record. Audit trail for change accountability.

Foreign key linking to experience.touchpoint. Business justification: Marketing survey programs are triggered at specific experience touchpoints (post-checkout, post-spa visit, post-dining). The touchpoint determines survey timing, content, and relevance. Critical for t

Foreign key linking to procurement.vendor. Business justification: Guest satisfaction surveys administered by third-party platforms (Medallia, Qualtrics). Linking survey program to vendor enables contract compliance tracking, invoice reconciliation, platform vendor p
Indicates whether the survey program is currently active and distributing surveys. True = active and sending surveys; False = inactive/paused.
Indicates whether low-score survey responses automatically create service recovery cases in the experience domain. True = auto-create cases; False = manual review required.
Timestamp when the survey program record was first created in the system. Audit trail for program lifecycle tracking.
Number of days to retain individual survey response data before anonymization or deletion. Aligns with GDPR data minimization and retention policies.

Primary channel through which the survey is delivered to guests: email (post-stay email), SMS (text message link), in-app (mobile app notification), web (website pop-up), kiosk (property self-service terminal), qr-code (printed QR code on receipt/collateral).
Date when the survey program stops distributing surveys. Null for open-ended programs. Trigger events after this date will not send surveys.
Date when the survey program begins distributing surveys. Trigger events occurring on or after this date will initiate survey sends.
Expected time in minutes for a guest to complete the survey. Displayed to guests to set expectations and improve response rates.
Description of the incentive offered for survey completion (e.g., '500 loyalty points', 'Entry into monthly $500 prize draw', '10% off next stay'). Null if incentive_offered_flag is False.
Indicates whether a reward or incentive is offered for survey completion (e.g., loyalty points, prize draw entry, discount code). True = incentive offered; False = no incentive.
Comma-separated list of ISO 639-1 language codes supported by this survey (e.g., 'en,es,fr,de,zh'). Enables multi-language guest experience.
Maximum number of reminder messages to send to a non-respondent. Null if reminder_enabled_flag is False. Typically 1-2 to avoid guest fatigue.
Timestamp when the survey program record was last modified. Audit trail for configuration change tracking.
Version identifier of the privacy notice presented to guests with this survey. Ensures GDPR/CCPA compliance and tracks consent framework changes.

Defines the property coverage of the survey program: all (enterprise-wide across all brands and properties), brand (specific brand segment - luxury/premium/select), region (geographic region), property (single property), portfolio (custom property grouping).
Total number of questions in the survey instrument. Used for survey length optimization and completion rate analysis.
Geographic region code when property_scope is 'region' (e.g., 'NA-EAST', 'EMEA', 'APAC'). Null for non-regional surveys.
Number of hours after initial survey send to wait before sending a reminder. Null if reminder_enabled_flag is False.
Indicates whether automated reminder messages are sent to non-respondents. True = reminders enabled; False = no reminders.
Number of hours to wait after the trigger event before sending the survey. Allows guests time to complete their experience (e.g., 24 hours post-checkout).
Score threshold below which a survey response triggers an automatic service recovery case in the experience domain (e.g., NPS detractor score <= 6, GSS score < 7). Enables real-time guest recovery.

Measurement framework category: NPS (Net Promoter Score - likelihood to recommend), GSS (Guest Satisfaction Score - overall satisfaction rating), CSAT (Customer Satisfaction - specific touchpoint satisfaction), transactional (event-triggered feedback), relationship (periodic loyalty/engagement survey).
Business name of the survey program (e.g., 'Post-Stay Guest Satisfaction Q1 2024', 'Luxury Segment NPS Wave 3').

Current lifecycle status of the survey program: draft (design phase, not yet launched), active (live and distributing), paused (temporarily suspended), completed (ended, no longer distributing), archived (historical record only).

Classification of the survey based on guest journey touchpoint: post-stay (after checkout), post-event (after MICE event completion), post-fnb (after Food and Beverage visit), loyalty (loyalty program member feedback), digital (website/app experience), pre-arrival (pre-stay expectations), in-stay (mid-stay pulse check). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: post-stay|post-event|post-fnb|loyalty|digital|pre-arrival|in-stay — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

URL template for the survey with placeholder tokens for personalization (e.g., 'https://survey.medallia.com/program/{program_id}?guest={guest_id}&res={reservation_id}'). Used for survey link generation.
Version identifier for the survey instrument (e.g., 'v2.1', '2024-Q1'). Tracks survey design iterations and question set changes for longitudinal analysis.
Target GSS score for the survey program (typically 0-100 or 0-10 scale). Used for goal setting and performance evaluation. Null for non-GSS surveys.
Target NPS score for the survey program (range -100 to +100). Used for goal setting and performance evaluation. Null for non-NPS surveys.
Target response rate percentage for the survey program (e.g., 25.00 for 25%). Used for performance tracking and optimization.
Guest segmentation criteria for survey targeting (e.g., 'Luxury Guests', 'Business Travelers', 'Loyalty Elite Members', 'Group Bookers', 'FIT Leisure'). Aligns with CRM segmentation taxonomy.

The operational event that initiates survey distribution (e.g., 'checkout', 'event_completion', 'fnb_visit', 'loyalty_tier_change', 'reservation_cancellation', 'complaint_resolution'). Maps to PMS or CRM event codes.
Primary key for survey_response

Foreign key linking to compliance.privacy_incident. Business justification: Survey responses contain guest PII and verbatim comments. If exposed in a data breach, linking survey responses to privacy incidents enables precise subject identification for mandatory GDPR/CCPA brea
Identifier of the guest who submitted this survey response. Links to the guest master data product.
Identifier of the hotel, resort, or property where the guest experience occurred that this survey response evaluates.
Identifier of the reservation or booking associated with this survey response. Nullable for non-stay surveys (e.g., event-only, F&B-only).
Identifier of the service recovery case created in response to this survey feedback. Nullable if no case was created.

Foreign key linking to guest.stay_history. Business justification: Survey responses must link to the exact stay_history record (not just reservation) to analyze NPS trends by actual room type delivered, service quality by specific staff on duty, and operational perfo
Identifier of the survey program or campaign under which this response was collected (e.g., Post-Stay NPS, Event Feedback, F&B Experience).
Channel through which the reservation was originally booked (e.g., Direct, OTA, GDS, Corporate). Used for satisfaction analysis by booking source.
Date when the guest checked out or completed their visit. Used to calculate survey timing and response lag.
Boolean flag indicating whether the guest granted permission to be contacted regarding their feedback. Required for GDPR and privacy compliance.
Timestamp when this survey response record was first created in the data product. Used for data lineage and audit trails.
Customer satisfaction score measuring satisfaction with specific touchpoints or services. May differ from GSS in scope and scale.
ISO 4217 three-letter currency code for the total spend amount (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP).
Boolean flag indicating whether this response requires follow-up action from guest services or management (typically true for detractors or critical feedback).
Overall guest satisfaction score, typically on a 0-100 scale or 1-5 scale depending on survey design. Measures overall satisfaction with the experience.
Classification of the guest's visit purpose (leisure, business, group, event). Used for satisfaction segmentation by travel purpose.
Number of nights the guest stayed at the property. Used for satisfaction correlation analysis with stay duration.
Guest's loyalty program tier at the time of the survey response (e.g., Silver, Gold, Platinum). Used for satisfaction analysis by loyalty segment.
External unique identifier assigned by the Medallia survey platform for this response. Used for cross-system reconciliation and audit trails.
Classification of the guest based on NPS score: Promoter (9-10), Passive (7-8), Detractor (0-6). Used for NPS calculation and segmentation.
Guest's likelihood-to-recommend rating on a 0-10 scale. Core metric for NPS calculation. 0=Not at all likely, 10=Extremely likely.
Code representing the rate plan or pricing tier under which the guest booked (e.g., BAR, Corporate, Loyalty Member). Used for satisfaction segmentation.
Channel through which the guest submitted the survey response (e.g., email link, SMS, mobile app, in-property kiosk).
Status indicating whether the survey response was fully completed, partially completed, or abandoned by the guest.
Number of hours elapsed between survey invitation sent and survey response submitted. Used for engagement analysis.
Date and time when the guest submitted the survey response. Represents the principal business event timestamp for this transaction.
Code representing the type of room the guest stayed in (e.g., Standard King, Deluxe Suite). Used for satisfaction analysis by room category.
Automated sentiment analysis classification of the verbatim comment. Derived from natural language processing of guest feedback text.
Numeric sentiment score derived from text analytics, typically ranging from -1.0 (very negative) to +1.0 (very positive).
Date of the guest stay or visit that this survey response evaluates. Used for temporal analysis and linking to operational data.
ISO 639-1 two-letter language code indicating the language in which the survey was presented and completed by the guest.
Date and time when the survey invitation was sent to the guest. Used to calculate response time and survey engagement metrics.
Version identifier of the survey template used for this response. Used for tracking survey design changes and ensuring consistent analysis.
Total amount spent by the guest during the stay or visit, including room, F&B, and ancillary charges. Used for satisfaction-revenue correlation analysis.
Timestamp when this survey response record was last modified. Used for change tracking and data quality monitoring.
Free-text comment provided by the guest explaining their rating or describing their experience. Critical for qualitative analysis and service recovery.

Unique identifier for the goods receipt transaction. Primary key.

Reference to the PIP project if this goods receipt is part of a capital improvement initiative. Links FF&E procurement to renovation projects.
Identifier of the manager or authorized person who approved the goods receipt for posting. Required for segregation of duties and SOX compliance.
Identifier of the hotel or resort property where goods are being received. Enables property-level inventory tracking.
Reference to the purchase order against which goods are being received. Links to the originating procurement document.
Identifier of the staff member who physically received and inspected the goods. Used for accountability and training purposes.
Identifier of the supplier or vendor delivering the goods. Used for vendor performance tracking and three-way match.
Indicates whether the received materials are tracked by batch or lot number. Essential for F&B traceability and recall management.
Shipping document number for freight shipments. Used for tracking and freight audit, especially for FF&E deliveries.
System timestamp when the goods receipt was cancelled or reversed. Used for exception tracking and process improvement analysis.

description: Classification of the procurement as capital expenditure (FF&E, PIP projects) or operating expenditure (supplies, consumables). Drives accounting treatment and financial reporting.
Physical condition assessment of goods upon delivery. Drives acceptance decisions, vendor performance scoring, and claims processing.

System timestamp when the goods receipt record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and process timing analysis.

Shipping and freight costs associated with this delivery. May be capitalized into inventory cost or expensed depending on accounting policy.

External business identifier for the goods receipt document. Unique alphanumeric code used in SAP MM for tracking and audit purposes.
Current lifecycle status of the goods receipt transaction. Tracks progression from draft through posting to potential reversal.
Quality inspection outcome for the received goods. Determines whether goods are accepted into inventory or flagged for return/credit.
System timestamp when the goods receipt record was last updated. Tracks changes for audit and compliance purposes.

Indicates whether any portion of the goods receipt was rejected due to quality issues. Triggers vendor notification and return processing.
The actual date when goods were physically received at the property. Used for inventory valuation and lead time analysis.
Free-text comments from the receiving staff regarding delivery conditions, discrepancies, or other observations. Used for dispute resolution and vendor feedback.
Indicates whether this goods receipt represents a return to vendor rather than an inbound delivery. Used for reverse logistics tracking.
Indicates whether the received items are tracked by individual serial numbers. Typical for FF&E assets and high-value equipment.
Notes regarding special handling requirements for the received goods, such as refrigeration, fragile items, hazardous materials, or security protocols.
Code identifying the specific storeroom, warehouse, or storage area within the property where goods are placed. Examples: main kitchen, housekeeping storage, central warehouse.
Indicates whether the goods require temperature-controlled storage. Critical for F&B inventory compliance with food safety regulations.
Status of the three-way match process comparing purchase order, goods receipt, and vendor invoice. Critical for accounts payable processing and internal controls.
Aggregate quantity of all line items received in this goods receipt transaction. Used for high-level receiving volume tracking.
Total monetary value of all goods received in this transaction, in the property's local currency. Used for inventory valuation and financial reporting.
Physical location at the property where goods were delivered and unloaded. Examples: loading dock, main entrance, kitchen entrance.
Monetary difference between expected value (from PO) and actual received value. Triggers investigation when exceeds tolerance thresholds.
Standardized code explaining the cause of any variance between PO and GR. Used for root cause analysis and process improvement.
Unique identifier for the material master record. Primary key for the material catalog.

Identifier of the user who created the material master record. Supports accountability and audit compliance.

Inventory classification based on value and usage frequency. A items are high-value/high-usage requiring tight control; C items are low-value/low-usage with relaxed management. Drives inventory policy and cycle count frequency.
List of allergens present in the material, critical for Food and Beverage (F&B) ingredients. Examples include dairy, gluten, nuts, shellfish, soy. Required for menu labeling and guest safety.

Commercial brand or trademark under which the material is marketed. Important for guest-facing amenities and Food and Beverage (F&B) ingredients where brand consistency impacts guest experience.

description: Indicates whether the material is capitalized as a fixed asset (CapEx) or expensed as an operating cost (OpEx). Furniture Fixtures and Equipment (FF&E) for Property Improvement Plan (PIP) projects are typically CapEx; consumables and supplies are OpEx.
Three-letter ISO 3166 country code indicating where the material is manufactured or sourced. Required for customs, trade compliance, and sustainability reporting.
Date and time when the material master record was first created in the system. Supports audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the standard price. Enables multi-currency procurement and financial consolidation.
Date after which the material is no longer available for procurement. Used for planned discontinuation and phase-out of materials.
Date from which the material master record is valid and available for procurement transactions. Supports phased rollout of new materials.
Indicates whether the material is classified as hazardous and requires special handling, storage, and disposal procedures per Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) regulations.
Average number of days from purchase order placement to goods receipt. Used for Material Requirements Planning (MRP) calculations and reorder point determination.
Name of the company that manufactures or produces the material. Used for quality tracking, warranty management, and vendor performance evaluation.
Unique part number assigned by the manufacturer. Enables precise identification for ordering, quality control, and warranty claims.
Full descriptive name of the material, supply, or good. Provides human-readable identification for procurement, receiving, and inventory management.
Detailed grouping of materials within a procurement category for granular spend analysis, vendor specialization, and contract management. Enables category-level reporting and sourcing strategy.
Unique alphanumeric code assigned to the material in the procurement system. External business identifier used across purchase orders, goods receipts, and inventory transactions.

Current lifecycle status of the material in the catalog. Active materials are available for procurement; inactive or discontinued materials are phased out; blocked materials cannot be ordered due to quality or compliance issues.
Classification of the material based on its procurement and usage purpose. Determines procurement rules, valuation methods, and inventory management policies.
Upper limit for inventory holding to prevent overstocking, minimize carrying costs, and reduce waste for perishable items.
Smallest quantity that can be ordered from vendors. Driven by vendor terms, packaging constraints, or economic order quantity calculations.
Date and time when the material master record was last updated. Tracks changes to pricing, status, vendor assignments, and other attributes.
Indicates whether the material has a limited shelf life and requires expiration date tracking. True for Food and Beverage (F&B) ingredients, fresh produce, and time-sensitive supplies.
Method by which the material is procured. Standard is direct purchase; consignment is vendor-owned inventory; subcontracting involves external processing; stock transfer is inter-property movement.
Unit in which the material is ordered from vendors. May differ from base unit (e.g., purchased in cases but stocked in each). Conversion factor to base unit is maintained separately.
Indicates whether incoming goods must undergo quality inspection before acceptance. True for critical materials, Food and Beverage (F&B) ingredients, and items with quality history issues.
Inventory level at which a replenishment order should be triggered. Calculated based on lead time demand and safety stock requirements to prevent stockouts.

Abbreviated description of the material for use in reports, purchase orders, and mobile applications where space is limited.
Standard cost or valuation price of the material in the base currency. Used for inventory valuation, budgeting, and variance analysis. Updated periodically based on procurement trends.
Required environmental conditions for storing the material to maintain quality and safety. Determines warehouse zone assignment and handling procedures.

Indicates whether the material meets sustainability or environmental certification standards such as Fair Trade, Rainforest Alliance, organic, or eco-label certifications. Supports corporate social responsibility and guest experience programs.
Tax category code for the material determining applicable sales tax, value-added tax (VAT), or goods and services tax (GST) rates. Varies by jurisdiction and material type.
Maximum temperature in Celsius allowed for proper storage of temperature-sensitive materials. Exceeding this threshold may compromise quality or safety.

Reference to the hotel or resort property for which this schedule is published.

Indicates whether this schedule is currently active. True for current and future schedules; false for archived or superseded schedules.
Indicates whether overtime hours are pre-approved for this schedule period. True if overtime is authorized; false if all hours must remain within standard limits.
Estimated labor cost as a percentage of forecasted revenue for this schedule period. Key metric for USALI labor cost reporting and GOP analysis.
Difference between total_actual_hours and total_scheduled_hours. Positive values indicate overtime or unplanned coverage; negative values indicate understaffing or no-shows.
Date and time of the most recent update to this schedule record.
Free-text notes or instructions from the manager regarding this schedule (e.g., 'High occupancy expected due to conference', 'Reduced staffing for low season').
Last calendar date covered by this schedule period.
First calendar date covered by this schedule period.
Date and time when this schedule was published and made visible to employees.
Descriptive name for the schedule (e.g., 'Front Desk Week 15 2024', 'Housekeeping Spring Break Schedule').
Human-readable business identifier for the schedule, typically formatted as property-department-period (e.g., HTL001-HSKP-2024W15).
Current lifecycle state of the schedule. Draft: under construction by manager. Published: visible to employees. Finalized: locked for payroll processing. Archived: historical record.

Classification of the schedule based on operational context: regular (standard weekly/biweekly), seasonal (high/low season adjustments), event (MICE or special events), emergency (unplanned coverage), training (learning and development sessions).
Sum of actual hours worked by all employees during this schedule period, captured from time and attendance systems. Used for labor variance reporting (actual vs. scheduled).

Full-time equivalent headcount for this schedule period, calculated as total_scheduled_hours divided by standard full-time hours (typically 40 hours per week). Used for labor productivity analysis and GOP (Gross Operating Profit) reporting.
Sum of all scheduled shift hours across all employees in this schedule. Used for labor cost forecasting and CPOR (Cost Per Occupied Room) labor planning.
Version number of this schedule. Incremented each time the schedule is revised before finalization. Supports schedule change tracking and audit trail.
